key_insight: Large language models can help abstract cancer registry variables from clinical records, with the strongest models performing best on simpler registry fields and less well on exact date extraction.
clinical_relevance: If validated in practice, these models could reduce manual registrar workload and speed cancer surveillance data entry across multiple cancer types.
method: Evaluated five foundation LLMs on real-world cancer registry abstraction tasks
Used zero-shot prompting on eight registry variables from clinical reports
Tested 5,939 patients across seven cancer types against hospital registry ground truth
evidence_signal: In a large evaluation, top models reached about F1 ~0.8 for low-cardinality variables, around 0.55 exact accuracy for diagnosis/treatment dates, and about 0.85 accuracy when a ±30-day tolerance was allowed.
watch_item: Performance varied by model and variable, so real deployment would still need human oversight, especially for high-stakes fields like exact dates and high-cardinality entries.
paper_title: Large language models for cancer registry abstraction: a real-world evaluation across models, variables, and cancer types
